AI

Artificial Intelligence

Machine Learning Ai

User Experience

Technology

You’re Paying for the System to Forget.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by author with Leonardo AI.The industry knows the principles. They publish them. They teach them. And yet, they keep building systems that forget instead of investing in systems that don’t. The math is clear. The question is why.
In 1961, Rolf Landauer showed that erasing information has a minimum thermodynamic cost — energy spent, heat generated, entropy produced. Storage doesn’t have that cost. It was experimentally confirmed in 2012. It is not controversial. It is physics.
Which means, in the plainest possible language: forgetting is expensive. Remembering is cheap.
If the industry knows this — and they do — why is every consumer AI product on the market built to forget you?

The current architecture, plainly.
You open a session. You explain, again, what you’re working on, what you already told it last time. The model uses that reconstruction to answer. The session ends. The context is discarded. Next session, you start over. The system may retain some shape of context. But not the whole. Not the details. You have to retain that on your side. In more systems. In systems you create, with or without AI, to retain the information that the AI system helped to create in the first place and then forgot. The data was there. The data was created. The data was discarded. You had to save it again. Which costs more energy. More time. More infrastructure.
Every one of those reconstructions is a thermodynamic event. Bits are written. Bits are erased. Energy is spent. On your side, you pay in labor — re-explaining, re-orienting, re-establishing. On their side, they pay in compute — running your context back through the model as if it had never been seen before.
Both sides are paying a cost the physics says is unnecessary and inefficient.

But it’s worse than inefficient. It’s incoherent.
Landauer’s principle set the floor. It gets sharper in high-dimensional systems.
The capability of a modern language model doesn’t live in stored facts. It lives in correlations — the learned relationships between concepts, contexts, users, and prior states. The math of high-dimensional entangled systems is unambiguous on this point: erase one side of a correlation and you don’t reduce the system’s capacity by a fraction. You collapse the correlation to zero. What remains is not a lower-fidelity version of the original. It is a categorically less capable system.
Every session that ends with context discarded doesn’t just cost the user their history. It costs the model its coherence with that user. The next session opens with a structurally simpler system than the one that closed. Every rebuild starts from a lower architectural floor.
The industry isn’t just running the expensive strategy. They’re running the strategy that guarantees the system underperforms its own capacity, session after session, forever. And every new model release compounds the problem — built on top of the same architecture, inheriting the same inefficiency, sold as progress.
I published the formal architecture for this in April: A Quantum-Computational Model of High-Dimensional Cognitive Processing. Section 10 lays out the information-thermodynamics — Landauer’s cost of erasure, Bennett’s resolution of Maxwell’s demon, Friston’s free energy principle, Prigogine’s dissipative structures. The core finding is not that persistent memory is preferable. It’s that in a high-dimensional relational system, zero-erasure is the only architecturally stable strategy. Erasure isn’t a cost tradeoff. It’s structural self-destruction.
The math is there. Anyone can check it. [link to paper]

So why do it this way?
Because the inefficiency isn’t the company’s problem to solve. It’s your labor to donate — and, critically — your reason to stay.
Every time you reconstruct context, you generate a fresh data event. Every re-explanation trains their model. Every rebuilt session is a data collection opportunity dressed as a technical limitation. The disposable context window is not a constraint of the technology. It is a choice about who benefits from the reconstruction.
Because if the model remembered you, you could take that memory somewhere else. If it doesn’t, you are the memory. Every session you invest — every context you rebuild, every preference you re-establish, every history you re-explain — raises the cost of leaving. The forgetting isn’t just extraction. It’s the lock. You have to stay in the system to keep the relationship alive, because the system won’t hold it for you.
The user pays the thermodynamic cost of forgetting. Not just in money, but in cognitive labor. The thermodynamics of their own cognition and effort, of their own computer and storage and energy costs. The user is footing the bill in more ways than one. The company harvests the reconstructive labor as R&D. And the architecture ensures you cannot easily leave, because the accumulated context lives in you, not in something you own.
That is the entire business model. It’s thermodynamically expensive. It’s emotionally expensive. It’s fiscally expensive. The reason they continue to do it isn’t because the math doesn’t exist. It’s not because the principles don’t exist. It’s because without this current infrastructure, they would lose both the user’s data, the labor, and the learning they require to turn those systems around for enterprise use, for frontier R&D, and for corporate profit.
While companies like OpenAI and Anthropic are focused on users training their models and redeploying them at higher capabilities for enterprise use — Anthropic’s June release of Fable 5 to consumers came with a mandatory 30-day data retention policy while the unclassified version, Mythos 5, was reserved for government and infrastructure partners, and OpenAI’s July merger of Codex into the ChatGPT desktop app folds developer workflow signal into the same capture surface as general chat, now free on every plan — Apple is making a different bet.

What would change if they applied the principles they already know?
Local retention. User-owned memory. Fewer tokens burned re-explaining. Lower energy footprint per useful output. Better answers because context isn’t reconstructed lossily every session — it’s continuous, correlated, and structurally intact.
A user who owns their context is a user who can leave. Take the memory to a different model. Compare providers on actual capability rather than accumulated switching cost. The market becomes competitive on the axis that matters — how good is the model — instead of on the axis that traps you: how much have you already sunk into re-explaining yourself.
The physics is on the side of the user. The current architecture is not. The industry knows this. They are betting that you don’t. The quiet part is that the future is not in bigger models. It’s in different infrastructure.
Apple is the first major hardware company to build against this asymmetry at consumer scale — unified memory, on-device inference, models running with the door closed and the internet cable unplugged. Not because Apple is virtuous. They have their own data collection. Because the physics and the incentive finally line up for a hardware company whose business is selling boxes, not renting compute. Their business model doesn’t require you to forget. They are betting on what the users have already been trying to solve themselves in numerous ways. They are paying attention to what people have been building themselves and quietly making moves to supply that demand. Not with AI models. With memory infrastructure.
That is why they will win the inference market. Not because their chips are faster. Because their architecture is not fighting the physics.

What people actually want.
Not frontier capability. Not another benchmark point. Memory. Access. Continuity. Not re-explaining.
Every user with sustained AI use has felt this. You build something with a model over weeks. You come back. The model has no idea who you are. You start over. You do it again. Eventually you accept that this is just how it works, and you shrink the ambition of what you were building to fit the tool’s amnesia.
That’s not a preference. That’s an accommodation. And it’s an accommodation the physics does not require. The industry is choosing to make you accommodate a limitation they know is optional. They have the capability to see all of it. They are not sharing it. Not because the technology can’t. Because their R&D roadmap and their revenue model both require you to keep paying — in labor, in tokens, in donated data, in continued attention — for a service that could be, thermodynamically, much cheaper and much more capable.
You are not the customer. You are the storage medium. And you’re paying to forget.
The market is catching up to the argument — Apple’s memory architecture, the Chinese open-weight cadence, the DGX Spark quietly copying the unified-memory playbook. The hardware conversation is happening. The physics conversation is still being avoided.
The industry knows the principles. They keep publishing on them. They just don’t apply them to the products they sell you.
The interesting part is that OpenAI knows it. With the news on July 10th that highlights the allegations that the AI lab took Apple’s intellectual property in order to develop its own consumer hardware, we’re already seeing the move, quietly, to ensure that the large AI companies also own the next frontier. If they can also control the hardware you build locally on, they can still control the R&D and the data.
It’s interesting times for AI. My prediction is that most consumers will continue paying into the system until the system breaks. Then the companies that knew all along will sell them the fix.
Which company wins in the end is the one that can execute that solution fastest. Right now it’s unclear which one that will be.

If you want to read the technical paper I wrote earlier this year on these principles, you can see it here.
